stories.headline: Two inmates dead after incident at Bent County Correctional Facility; CDOC suspends statewide prison visitation
stories.summary:
Two inmates died and a third was hospitalized following an incident Friday night at the Bent County Correctional Facility in southeastern Colorado, the Department of Corrections announced Sunday. The facility was placed on lockdown, and CDOC suspended visitation at all 21 of its facilities statewide as a precautionary measure.

No staff members were injured in the incident, and officials say there is no threat to surrounding communities. The CDOC Office of the Inspector General is leading the investigation, and the department said additional details would not be released until next-of-kin notifications are complete.

The statewide visitation freeze affects inmates and their families across Colorado's entire prison system until further notice. CDOC described the suspension as a precautionary step intended to support department operations and maintain safety and security.

stories.headline: One dead, four injured in series of overnight shootings across Denver metro
stories.summary:
Denver police are investigating four separate shootings that occurred Saturday night into Sunday morning, injuring four people. The incidents included a shooting around 10:48 p.m. Saturday in the 4700 block of Airport Way, where a victim was hospitalized and 36-year-old Davon Morgan was subsequently arrested on suspicion of first-degree assault.

Separately, a 25-year-old man was fatally shot in a northwest Aurora alley in the early hours of Sunday morning, according to Aurora police. That homicide is being investigated by Aurora authorities and is among five separate weekend shootings across the Denver metro area.

In an unrelated incident in Arvada, a man was arrested after allegedly stabbing both of his parents just after noon Saturday in the 8500 block of Union Circle. Anyone with information on the Denver shootings is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: One killed in suspected drunk driving crash in Aurora early Saturday
stories.summary:
A male passenger died following a two-car crash at the intersection of South Peoria Street and East Jewell Avenue in Aurora around 3 a.m. Saturday, according to Aurora police. The passenger was transported to a hospital with critical injuries and later pronounced dead; both drivers were also hospitalized with non-life-threatening injuries.

Investigators believe a Hyundai SUV was making a southbound turn from westbound Jewell when it collided with a southbound Nissan Xterra traveling through the intersection. Both drivers exhibited signs of alcohol consumption, and intoxication is being investigated as a possible contributing factor.

The identity of the deceased passenger will be released by the Arapahoe County Coroner's Office. The crash is under active investigation by Aurora police.

stories.headline: Polis vetoes credit card swipe fee bill, immigration lawsuit measure, and firefighter cancer coverage
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis vetoed three additional bills last week, including Senate Bill 134, which would have prohibited credit card companies from charging businesses a swipe fee on the sales tax portion of transactions. Polis said he supports the bill's core concept but cited legal risk, implementation concerns, and the likelihood that the measure could be tied up in litigation as reasons for the veto, pointing to ongoing legal battles that have repeatedly delayed a similar law in Illinois.

Polis also vetoed Senate Bill 005, which would have allowed individuals to sue immigration officers for alleged constitutional violations, and a third measure related to firefighter cancer benefits. The vetoes bring Polis's total for the session to at least 12, setting a personal record.

The Electronic Payments Coalition, which represents banks, credit unions, and card networks, praised the swipe fee veto, while bill sponsor Majority Leader Monica Duran argued the fees unfairly burden small businesses. The governor's veto string has drawn attention from both business groups and progressive advocates.

stories.headline: Kyle Freeland sets Rockies innings-pitched record as Brewers complete sweep with 12-4 win
stories.summary:
Denver native Kyle Freeland surpassed Aaron Cook on Sunday to become the all-time franchise leader in innings pitched as a Colorado Rockie, reaching 1,313 1/3 innings during a fifth-inning flyout against Milwaukee. Freeland, a Thomas Jefferson High School graduate and the Rockies' first-round pick in 2014, received a standing ovation from the Coors Field crowd when the milestone was displayed on the scoreboard.

The milestone was a bright spot in an otherwise difficult afternoon. The Brewers completed a three-game sweep with a 12-4 victory, pounding Colorado for 17 hits including 10 extra-base hits. The loss dropped the Rockies to 24-42, putting them 18 games below .500 for the first time this season and on pace to lose more than 100 games.

Freeland pitched five-plus innings, allowing three runs on seven hits, and trimmed his ERA from 8.06 to 7.81. Manager Warren Schaeffer praised Freeland's durability and resilience in setting the franchise record, while Freeland acknowledged he still needs to improve his performance on the mound.

stories.headline: Update: Broncos linebacker Jonathon Cooper issues public apology following domestic violence arrest
stories.summary:
Broncos outside linebacker Jonathon Cooper posted a public apology on social media Saturday night following his arrest on domestic violence charges earlier in the week. "I apologize to my family, to my friends and my community. This situation is not who I am," Cooper wrote, also sharing a Bible verse referencing anger.

Cooper, 28, was booked into Douglas County Jail early Friday morning after an incident allegedly occurring around 11:15 p.m. Thursday. According to an affidavit obtained by the Denver Gazette, his girlfriend accused him of cheating, leading to a dispute over a cell phone during which Cooper allegedly braced his neck against hers. He faces two domestic violence charges and one count of criminal mischief and was released on a $1,000 personal recognizance bond.

The Broncos have stated they are gathering more information. Cooper's attorney Harvey Steinberg has not responded to media requests. The team is scheduled to resume organized team activities Tuesday through Thursday at Broncos Park.

stories.headline: Colorado native Becca Huffer wins third Inspirato Colorado Women's Open title, takes home $50,000
stories.summary:
Colorado native Becca Huffer claimed her third title at the Inspirato Colorado Women's Open on Friday, earning a $50,000 check at the Green Valley Ranch Golf Club in Denver. The win adds to Huffer's legacy at the regional tournament, which has become a showcase for some of the best stories in Colorado golf.

Huffer joins a list of players who have raved about the quality and atmosphere of the event, which has grown into one of the more prominent women's professional golf tournaments in the region. Her three titles at the event are the most by any competitor in the tournament's history.

The Colorado Women's Open continues to attract national attention while providing opportunities for professional golfers in the region. This year's field competed at Green Valley Ranch, a public course northeast of Denver.

stories.headline: Colorado governor declares statewide drought emergency as state enters Phase 3 conditions
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis declared a statewide drought emergency as Colorado entered Phase 3 drought conditions, reflecting the severity of an ongoing water crisis across the state. The declaration signals a significant escalation in the state's official response to what officials describe as historic drought conditions.

The emergency declaration comes as Colorado faces unprecedented interstate conflicts over dwindling water supplies, particularly along the Colorado River system, and as the state grapples with the compounding effects of climate change on snowpack and precipitation. Phase 3 drought conditions represent a severe tier in the state's classification system.

The declaration is expected to unlock additional state resources and emergency powers to manage water usage and conservation across Colorado. It follows a recently signed federal plan imposing a 10-year Colorado River management framework with mandatory renegotiations.

stories.headline: Colorado researchers explore drought-resistant crops as potential solution to state's deepening water crisis
stories.summary:
With agriculture consuming up to 90% of Colorado's available water annually, researchers at Colorado State University's crop-testing station near Akron are testing drought-resistant alternatives such as black-eyed peas, sainfoin, rye grass, camelina, and sunflowers that require far less irrigation than traditional crops like alfalfa. Growing alfalfa in Burlington requires 44 inches of water per year, compared to just 15 inches for black-eyed peas, highlighting the potential for significant water savings.

The Colorado Sun is launching a solutions journalism series examining whether crop-switching and other conservation strategies can free up water at a meaningful scale. The series will assess efforts from Akron to Aspen and evaluate whether local conservation measures can outpace the pressures of natural drought cycles and interstate water conflicts.

Farmers across the Eastern Plains and Western Slope express willingness to adapt, but market uncertainty remains a central obstacle. Growers who pivot to alternative crops need guaranteed buyers at harvest time, and past experiments — including a glut of unsold hemp bales — underscore the financial risks of changing course without stable demand.

stories.headline: Colorado appeals court orders retrial for paramedics convicted in Elijah McClain's death
stories.summary:
The Colorado Court of Appeals has ordered a new trial for the paramedics convicted in connection with the 2019 death of Elijah McClain, the Aurora man who died after being injected with ketamine by Aurora Fire Rescue paramedics following a police encounter. The ruling represents a significant legal development in one of Colorado's highest-profile cases in recent years.

McClain, 23, died after Aurora police stopped him while he was walking home and called paramedics, who administered ketamine. His death sparked widespread protests in 2020 and led to criminal charges against both officers and paramedics involved in the encounter.

The appeals court's decision means the paramedics will face a new trial, prolonging a legal proceeding that has already spanned years. The ruling adds another chapter to a case that prompted major reforms in Aurora's police and fire departments and drew national attention to the use of chemical sedation by first responders.

stories.headline: Romance-only bookstore and cafe to open this summer along South Broadway in Englewood
stories.summary:
Meg Garcia-Jahrman, a 29-year-old Morrison native, is preparing to open Good Girl Cafe and Bookstore at 2807 S. Broadway in Englewood, a romance-only bookstore and coffee shop expected to debut this summer. Garcia-Jahrman said the concept was inspired by more than 400 romance novels she read while grieving the deaths of both of her parents — who died from heart-related conditions one year apart in September 2024 and 2025 — and by the comfort she found in local coffee shops during that period.

The store will feature dark green walls, vintage furniture, and shelves dedicated entirely to romance titles ranging from contemporary fiction to romantasy, along with a section highlighting disability representation. The cafe will serve specialty beverages using coffee from Subjective Coffee.

Garcia-Jahrman said an inheritance from her parents made the business financially possible. She has documented the buildout extensively on social media since April, drawing a following ahead of the opening. Good Girl joins a growing national trend of genre-specific bookstores emerging amid surging interest in romance fiction.

stories.headline: Nearly half of PERA investment staff doubled salaries through incentive pay, Colorado Sun analysis finds
stories.summary:
A Colorado Sun analysis found that nearly half of the investment staff at the Public Employees' Retirement Association of Colorado effectively doubled their base salaries through incentive compensation. PERA manages retirement benefits for hundreds of thousands of Colorado public employees, including teachers, state workers, and local government employees.

The findings raise questions about compensation structures at the state's largest public pension fund at a time when PERA has faced scrutiny over its long-term funding levels. Incentive pay at public pension funds is common practice nationally, with proponents arguing it is necessary to recruit and retain investment talent competitive with the private sector.

The analysis does not indicate wrongdoing, but the scale of the incentive payouts is drawing attention from observers who follow public employee benefits and government spending in Colorado. PERA oversees tens of billions of dollars in assets on behalf of its members.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Take the crayons out of your nose, they don't go there. Why do I even have to tell you these things?

topStory.headline: Israel and Iran Exchange Direct Strikes; Trump Demands Halt as Iran Announces Suspension of Operations
topStory.summary:
Israel and Iran traded direct military strikes Monday in the worst escalation since a ceasefire took effect in April, with the Israeli Defense Forces conducting strikes on military targets in western and central Iran and Iran launching missile barrages toward Israel in retaliation. The exchanges marked the first direct military confrontation between the two countries since the April truce and were triggered by Israeli airstrikes on Hezbollah strongholds in southern Beirut, Lebanon. Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi forces also fired a missile toward Israel during the escalation.

Iran subsequently announced a suspension of armed forces operations, with its central military headquarters, the Khatam al-Anbiya Central HQ, stating that Israel had received "heavy, targeted, intelligent, and damaging strikes" in support of Lebanon. The statement warned, however, that "far more severe and crushing measures" would follow if Israeli operations in southern Lebanon continued. The Israeli Prime Minister's Office said Israel would not accept any equation in which strikes on Hezbollah targets in Beirut were met with Iranian attacks on Israeli population centers.

President Donald Trump publicly called on both sides to stop shooting and later stated the two countries were moving toward a ceasefire, insisting a broader diplomatic deal was close. A U.S. official said Trump had urged Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to hold off on retaliating against Iran before the escalation intensified. Notably, Iran did not strike American military bases in the region, did not attack shipping lanes broadly, and did not fire on Gulf state targets such as Dubai or Kuwait, which a senior U.S. military official told NBC News may signal Tehran's desire to avoid a wider war involving the United States and its Gulf allies.

The escalation unfolded against a backdrop of ongoing conflict rooted in the broader regional war. According to ABC News, President Trump announced major joint U.S.-Israeli combat operations against Iran in February, and initial peace talks in Pakistan in April failed to produce a deal, after which Trump declared an open-ended ceasefire extension and continued a U.S. blockade pending negotiations. A central sticking point remains Lebanon: Iran has demanded that any peace agreement cover Lebanon, home to its proxy force Hezbollah, while the United States and Israel have insisted Lebanon be treated as a separate matter.

Markets reacted sharply to the developments before partially recovering. U.S. crude oil futures surged as much as 5.4% before retreating to a gain of roughly 1.5% after Iran announced its suspension of operations, while stock futures moved higher on the same news. Separately, a U.S.-sanctioned oil tanker, the Motor Tanker Marivex, was reported to be on fire and sinking off the coast of Oman, with its crew of 24 Indian seafarers issuing a distress call claiming a U.S. Navy missile strike; U.S. Central Command had not responded to CNN's request for comment. Whether Monday's exchanges represent an isolated spike in hostilities or the beginning of a broader collapse of the April ceasefire remained unclear.
